Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study at specific times, but I mostly just ended up scrolling through course materials whenever I felt like it, which wasn't often. I'd usually wait until I felt some pressure building up before actually sitting down to focus. Most of my 16 hours a week happened in random bursts rather than any planned schedule.
Q2: My pattern was definitely deadline-driven. I'd be pretty relaxed and barely touch anything for days, then suddenly realize something was due and cram everything in at once. Regular weekly study times? That's a nice idea, but it never really stuck for me. Life stuff and my general lack of motivation made it hard to commit to a consistent schedule.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or just pure boredom, ironically. I'd log in, scroll around, maybe watch a video or two without really absorbing much. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even when I wasn't really engaging with the content. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more than I would have otherwise.
Q4: There was a brief period where checking the LMS became somewhat automatic, especially when the gamified elements were introduced. Earning points or completing little challenges gave me something to do when I was bored. But I wouldn't say it ever became a deeply ingrained habit—more like a distraction that happened to be educational sometimes.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could watch them while doing other things, pause and rewind when needed. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things into smaller, less overwhelming pieces. Anything that felt like less of a chore was easier for me to actually complete.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums most of the time. Posting felt awkward and I never knew what to say that hadn't already been said. The heavy reading materials also got pushed aside a lot—I'd download them with good intentions but rarely actually read through them. It all just felt tedious and I couldn't make myself care enough.
Q7: If something was quick, visual, and had some kind of immediate payoff, I'd actually do it. The gamified tasks worked because they felt less like schoolwork and more like checking boxes in a game. Anything that dragged on forever or felt pointless made me zone out completely. I needed to see the point right away or I'd lose interest.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, honestly. Required ones at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them even when I was bored out of my mind. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe later' and later never came. The stakes just weren't there to push me past my natural resistance.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them on my tablet away from the LMS. I'd screenshot slides, save PDFs, and review them during commutes or when I was lying in bed. I also used YouTube videos and other external sites to understand concepts that the course materials didn't explain well enough for me visually.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing downloaded notes or rewatching saved videos without being logged in. I'd also spend time just thinking through concepts while doing other things, like walking or cooking. None of that shows up anywhere, but it was still studying in my own way. The LMS only captures a fraction of what I actually did.
Q11: Definitely. I'd open a video once, download it or take screenshots of key frames, and then never open it on the LMS again. Same with PDFs—one download and then I'd study from my local copy. It was just more convenient than logging in every time, especially when my anxiety was high and the platform felt overwhelming.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. My anxiety spiked and everything felt pointless and exhausting. I'd stare at the screen feeling completely disconnected from the material, like it had nothing to do with my actual life. The boredom was almost physical—I just couldn't make myself care no matter how hard I tried.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or tired, the LMS felt like this massive obstacle I couldn't face. I'd log in, see all the things I needed to do, and immediately feel overwhelmed. Competing responsibilities like work and personal stuff made it easy to justify putting coursework on the back burner. Fatigue especially killed any motivation I might have had.
Q14: So many times. I'd have videos playing but be completely zoned out, thinking about other things or just staring blankly. I'd scroll through materials without actually reading them, just going through the motions. It looked like activity but my brain was elsewhere entirely. High engagement numbers don't mean I was actually learning.
Q15: I procrastinated constantly on anything that felt boring or difficult. Even knowing something was important, I'd convince myself I'd do it later when I felt more motivated—which rarely happened. The anxiety made it worse because starting felt overwhelming, so I'd avoid it until the deadline forced my hand. It's a frustrating cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore everything until a few days before something was due, then panic and rush through it. The LMS became very active right before deadlines and pretty dead otherwise. Without that external pressure, I'd just drift along doing the bare minimum.
Q17: Feedback helped when it was encouraging, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage even more. If I got a bad grade, my first instinct was to avoid thinking about the course entirely for a while. Good feedback gave me a temporary boost, but it never lasted long enough to change my overall patterns.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty standard, which meant it was easy to fall into passive patterns. The gamified elements were a nice change that actually got me participating more than I would have otherwise. But overall, the structure didn't really push me to engage deeply—it was easy to coast through doing the minimum.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt less like traditional schoolwork. But the sheer volume of content was overwhelming and made it harder to know where to focus. Clearer priorities and more interactive visual content would have helped me stay engaged instead of feeling lost in a sea of materials.
Q20: That's basically me sometimes. I'd be clicking around, watching videos, exploring different sections—but not actually producing anything. It's easy to feel busy without being productive. Sometimes you're trying to understand something before you can write about it, or you're just procrastinating in a way that looks like work.
Q21: That also sounds familiar. I studied a lot offline with downloaded materials, so my LMS activity didn't reflect everything I was doing. Some people just learn better independently and don't need to be constantly logged in. The system doesn't capture studying that happens outside its walls.
Q22: Logs can show when you're active and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss offline studying, mental engagement, and the quality of learning. Someone could have low activity but deep understanding, or high activity but complete confusion. The numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and curious. For me, real engagement is rare because I struggle with boredom and finding relevance. When I'm truly engaged, time passes without me noticing.
Q24: The LMS can't show my constant internal battle between anxiety and boredom, or how hard it is to make myself care about material that feels disconnected from my life. It doesn't capture the offline studying, the mental processing, or the emotional exhaustion that affects everything I do. My engagement is complicated in ways that clicks and timestamps can't measure.
